A New Studio Era: How Netflix is Becoming a Threat to the Film Industry By Al Kasper An undergraduate honors thesis submitted in partial fulfillment of the requirements for the degree of Bachelor of Arts in University Honors and Film Thesis Advisor Dr. Nancy Breaux Portland State University 2022 Introduction Technology has come a long way since the invention of the moving picture. The film industry has been a consistent source of entertainment since the early twentieth century and has evolved alongside public interest and technology. From the beginning, cinema was a large and profitable industry that grew rapidly. Unfortunately, a lack of strong regulations made it easy for larger studios to take over and essentially run the industry for a few decades.
At the beginning of the film industry was a time period known as the Studio Era. This era lasted from the 1920s to the 1950s and was dominated by major motion picture studios. The studios in question were Paramount Pictures, MGM, RKO, Twentieth Century Fox, and Warner Brothers. These studios managed to hold an oligarcial power over other studios due to their antitrust business practices. There were two practices in particular that led to the downfall of the Studio Era: vertical integration and block booking.
Vertical integration can be defined as when a company owns all stages of production.
This business practice is not unheard of in the business world, however, in the film industry, the use of this practice creates little to no competition. The major studios had the resources to own and operate their production, distribution, and exhibition of their films while smaller studios and independent studios struggled to compete. However, “the market control that resulted from bigness as such was not typically considered an offense” (Jacobs, 1983), which left these smaller studios struggling to profit while major competitors controlled most of the market.
A larger concern was block booking. Block booking was a practice that involved theaters purchasing a group (or block) of films to show rather than purchasing them separately. If a theater wanted to purchase a major motion picture, they were also required to purchase and play a set of B films that accompanied the major film. This created an even larger divide between the 2 major studios and smaller or independent studios. With the ability to buy out a whole theater, there was very little chance for independent productions to be screened to an audience.
Both of these practices got the attention of the Department of Justice and in 1948, a lawsuit was filed against the five major studios for participating in anti-trust business practices.
The decision ultimately concluded that the studios could no longer practice block booking and had to divorce from their theater ownerships in order to promote fair competition and a non-monopolistic industry. The US. versus Paramount Pictures' decision became known as the Paramount Decrees.
While this decision was made almost 75 years ago, it was repealed in 2019 due to the Department of Justice acknowledging the changes in technology and regulations, firmly believing that there should be no concern for future antitrust practices. Nevertheless, the streaming giant Netflix may prove to be a threat to the current structure of the film industry with or without the Paramount Decrees. This paper hopes to evaluate the future of the film industry in regards to how streaming services are beginning to create a monopolistic industry and the potential to enter a modernized Studio Era.
The Beginning Netflix has been an industry leader in at-home movie services for over 20 years. Founded in 1997 as a movie rental company, its biggest competitor at the time was Blockbuster, which refused to purchase Netflix despite the offer. Eventually, Netflix changed its service to include a new method of movie renting: the subscription model. This model provided customers with the ability to rent unlimited movies for a monthly subscription price. Blockbuster ultimately filed for bankruptcy, leaving Netflix as the winner of the two rental companies. (Sim, 2016).
3 In 2007, Netflix switched from a physical movie rental service to an online movie distributor. Works became even more available as customers could rent movies within minutes due to how Netflix “[p]partnered with manufacturers of various consumer electronics products, including video game consoles and Blu-ray Disc players, in order to enable its videos to be streamed over an Internet connection to those devices” (Hosch, 2022). Eventually, Netflix became a globally known service, no longer limited by physical mail; all movies could be streamed through customers’ personal devices connected to the internet.
Netflix released its first Netflix Original content in 2013 with the show House of Cards, which had about 2% of all Netflix subscribers watching upon its release (Rawden, 2014). The major streaming service has since created and offered over 2,400 original titles to its subscribers (Hosch, 2022). Netflix’s influence eventually spread to other streaming services such as Hulu and Amazon Prime, both also creating their own productions. “Netflix is aggressively creating original programming, whose global distribution rights Netflix will own outright” (Aguiar and Waldfogel, 2018), which could become a problem for both Hollywood and independent producers alike.
Independent Productions Independent film studios have historically struggled to compete against larger Hollywood studios.Independent studios were one of the largest groups demanding an end to block booking before the Paramount Decrees, as it denied them access to the most profitable parts of the industry (Jacobs, 1983). There has been consistent favorability in Hollywood studios over independent studios, especially in the distribution portion of the film industry (Gil, 2010) due to 4 their higher economic power to both produce higher budget films and own the three stages of the film industry.
In an effort to promote more equal competition within the film industry, the Department of Justice managed to ban block booking and end vertical integration with the Paramount Decrees that major studios had taken advantage of. By doing so, the audience had more access to independent and smaller studio productions than before, increasing the audience’s choices of films and therefore increasing studio competition.
In the modern day film industry, movie theaters have become less of an attraction, with most customers choosing to stay home and stream movies instead. With competition for theatergoers as a lesser issue, many independent productions now look towards services like Netflix to promote their content to a larger audience. There have been a number of benefits to streaming for independent studios, including that subscribers of Netflix actually rated indie productions higher than blockbusters in early feedback (Zu Tang, 2014). Another benefit for independent studios is that Netflix benefits from the less expensive licensing agreements for independent films given they meet the preferences of the company’s subscribers.
Netflix has taken an interesting position related to independent productions. Through the Sundance Film Festival, Netflix began acquiring the rights to multiple indie films that lead to its fifth nomination for an Oscar award (Morrison, 2017). These acts of acquisition beg the question of whether Netflix truly sees potential in independent productions or simply uses them for their own gain (award nominations and clout). Nevertheless, when Netflix decided to submit their own film to the Sundance festival, while the audience laughed at the Netflix logo playing on the screen (Morrison, 2017), independent studios realized a new competitor stood before them. With 5 the budget and resources that Netflix has available, there becomes less chance for lower-budget indie films to make their name heard. The Cannes Film Festival is an iconic opponent against Netflix, arguing that unless its films are debuted in a theater for a certain amount of time, the major studio cannot enter the competition, allowing space for independent productions to compete without the threat of Netflix to take the glory.
Hollywood Studios and Contract Competitiveness Hollywood studios are new to being the underdog in the film industry. They have been around for almost a century prior to the conception of streaming services, yet are struggling to keep up with the audience’s preferences of staying home to watch their choice of movies. These major studios were originally able to contract with Netflix and have their films available for rent, however, with Netflix changing into its own production studio, these Hollywood studios have serious competition.
From the beginning, Netflix proved to be a threat to Hollywood studios. With Hollywood making most of its money in the early 2000s through movie theater attendance and DVD purchases, the sudden emergence of DVD rental companies proved to be detrimental to their income. Companies like Netflix and Redbox took advantage of the First Sale Doctrine in an effort to undermine the Hollywood studios and be able to rent their titles without proper permission. The doctrine stated, “once a person buys a copyrighted item from the owner, the seller no longer has the right to prevent the seller from reselling it, giving it away, or lending it out” (Sullivan, 2010). This meant that Netflix and Redbox could simply purchase DVDs outright and rent them out to their customers with no legal ramifications.
6 This potentially unethical business practice did eventually disappear when Netflix turned toward digital content. Currently, according to the Netflix website, the company enters licensing agreements for titles and must remove them from its site once the agreement has expired. The site claims that when considering renewing a licensing agreement, areas to consider are whether the rights are still available, how popular the title is, and the cost of renewing the license. If the company decides that the renewal is not worth the extra costs, they will remove the title. This is considerably different than when they were renting out DVDs to customers. Rather than purchasing a copy outright, Netflix is now forced to consider numerous qualities of a title before offering it to their customers.
How does this impact Hollywood?
These licensing agreements not only limit Netflix and other streaming services to what they are allowed to stream but also encourage studios and streaming companies to make contracts limiting their competition. Netflix itself is particularly interested in gathering exclusive rights to the content that other streaming services want access to (Auigar and Waldfogel, 2018).
These rights allow them to be the only source to find certain content, which may attract customers who are specifically seeking those shows or movies. Some production studios are fearful of this tactic in that it may “[give] Netflix too much leverage in future negotiations'' (Auigar and Waldfogel, 2018).
Such contracts can already be seen occurring across streaming platforms. In 2011, “Netflix [had]... announced streaming deals with Warner Bros., Universal, Paramount, MGM, Lionsgate, Fox, NBC, ABC, Disney, MTV, and a host of indies” (Smith, 2011). As of 2021, Sony Pictures and Netflix entered an exclusive agreement for five years once the pictures leave 7 theaters, according to the New York Times. While this limits Sony Pictures’ ability to sell off the rights to their films to other streaming services, Netflix now has exclusive rights to Sony Picture films, making their content more diverse and sought after by subscribers.
Other streaming services are following suit. When Disney Plus was established, Disney pulled all of its content from its competition to keep exclusive rights to its content. Amazon Prime Video reached an exclusive deal with the Hollywood studio MGM, according to Business Insider. Many other studios are also using the concept of exclusive content to bring in new subscribers or interested customers to their service. (Clement, et al, 2020).
While these exclusive contracts are beneficial to streaming services by providing unique content, Hollywood studios are taking a hit. By signing these exclusivity agreements, Hollywood studios are forcibly limiting themselves to selling their content to one service. Cons of this type of contracting include limitations in competitive marketing as well as the potential to misvalue content that is signed over (Sanson and Steirer, 2019). Companies like Netflix have the ability to now undermine the competitive market with the appeal of the Paramount Decrees.
Netflix and the End of the Decrees For nearly ten years, Netflix has stood as its own production studio with consistent movies and TV shows known as Netflix Originals. The first Netflix Original was the show House of Cards in 2013. Concerned about the increasing expenses of licensing agreements, Netflix decided to enter the field as a production studio to save the company money and gain control over some of its content (Moore, 2016). Netflix becoming a studio has since encouraged other movie streaming platforms (Hulu, Amazon Prime, etc.) to join in the production portion of 8 the film industry. Now traditional studios are struggling to compete with the major distributors that push their own content before any other.
Even before the appeal of the Paramount Decrees, Netflix was seen as a threat to the film industry through an attempt to purchase Landmark Theaters ``causing many to wonder if Netflix was even able to buy a theater chain under the ' ‘Paramount Decrees’” (Oler, 2020). The company was already a production and distribution powerhouse, and with the purchase of a theater chain, would have landed themselves in a version of the vertical integration system banned in 1948. The company did manage to purchase The Paris in New York, officially breaking the Paramount Decrees before they were appealed.
These acts in defiance of the industry standards and regulations place Netflix as a difficult competitor for Hollywood and independent studios. Not only does Netflix now have the advantage of reintroducing vertical integration, there are other aspects of the company that threaten the competition of traditional studios. Compared to Hollywood and independent studios, Netflix has been positioned to “engage with fans' tastes in ways that the traditional industry cannot” (Burroughs, 2018).
The company has specific algorithms in place to suggest content to subscribers based on previous watched content. Contrary to the old system where movie audiences choose which movie based on marketing and their own interests, Netflix’s algorithm simplifies the movie watcher’s choices by suggesting movies or content that fits into the subscribers preferences.
(Hallinan and Strippas, 2016). A concern about this algorithmic switch relative to the film industry itself is the cultural differences that must be accounted for by such a large, global company. Will Netflix Original movies adapt to the censorship laws in certain countries and 9 create a barrier to accessibility with non censored content, or will Netflix manage to fill the gap between more and less heavily censored countries? With the Paramount Decrees (an American law for the American company) out of the way, Netflix now has the ability to fully integrate their company as production, distribution, and even potentially an exhibitor if they so wish.
The concern regarding the violation of the Paramount Decrees has become more than just a passing thought in recent years. The US Department of Justice repealed the Decrees in November of 2019, determining that both the technological advancements of the film industry and the modern regulations in place would prevent and discourage film studios from violating antitrust laws. The most major change endorsing the repeal for the Department of Justice was the change from theatrical views to streaming.
There are many in the film industry that frown upon the decision to repeal the Paramount Decrees, concerned it could revert back to monopolistic business practices, hurting the whole of the industry rather than supporting it. Dawson Oler wrote that the Department of Justice “[intervened] and [risked] a critical upset of the careful balance already in place” (Oler, 2020) in a long examination of the importance of the Paramount Decrees. Now that Netflix has become vertically integrated (owning one theater and both production and distribution), the resurgence of block booking is expected once the “sunset period” has ended. This “sunset period” refers to a 2 year gap between when the Decrees were appealed and when block booking is set to be legal again (unless action is taken against it). In regards to the return of block booking, it is currently unclear what that will look like and what the effects will be. For now, vertical integration is the only part of the Decrees that has been re-legalised, which in turn will allow studios to purchase theaters and become much more strict on the movies they show.
10 Conclusion The end of the Paramount Decree has left an unclear path for the film industry. Created in 1948 to discourage monopolistic business practices, the return of vertical integration and block booking have left the Directors Guild of America and many others worried about the threat that Netflix imposes on traditional studios. A potential new wave Studio Era is to be expected with the return of these practices and the rise of new industry leaders like Netflix, Hulu, and Amazon Prime. Both Hollywood and independent studios are expected to struggle against the global film powerhouse studios that rely on algorithms and push their own content to subscribers. However the end of the Decrees affects the film industry, one thing is clear: the success of online streaming and subscription services will outlast traditional studios.

# How Netflix and Streaming Services Are Changing The Film Industry

The entertainment landscape has changed dramatically in the last decade, with streaming platforms like **Netflix** and **Amazon Prime** becoming major players in creating blockbuster movies and hit TV series. This shift isn’t just about convenience—it’s about control. Big-name actors, directors, and producers are now turning to streaming platforms (and even starting their own production companies) to escape the traditional Hollywood studio system, especially in the wake of movements like **#MeToo**, which exposed widespread issues within the industry.

## 🎥 **Netflix Originals: Redefining the Blockbuster**

Netflix didn’t just want to be a platform that streams movies—it wanted to *make* them. Over the years, it’s produced films and TV shows featuring some of the biggest names in Hollywood, proving it can rival traditional studios. Here are some standout examples:

### **Movies**

## 🎬 **Hollywood’s Traditional Route: Big Studios and Blockbusters**

While streaming has disrupted the industry, traditional Hollywood still holds sway with mega-franchises and high-budget productions. Here are some examples of movies created the old-fashioned way:

## 🌟 **The #MeToo Movement and the Rise of Independent Production Companies**

The **#MeToo** movement, which gained momentum in 2017, exposed systemic abuse and harassment in Hollywood. Many actors and filmmakers began rethinking their relationships with major studios, opting for more creative—and safer—environments. This led to a significant shift, with stars starting their own production companies or partnering with streaming services. Here are some notable examples:

### **Big Stars Taking Control**

## 🎭 **Why Are Big Stars Jumping to Streaming?**

Streaming platforms are known for giving creators more control over their projects. For example:

### 2. **Better Deals and Ownership**

Actors and producers can negotiate better deals with streaming platforms, often retaining ownership rights to their projects. For instance:

### 3. **Avoiding Industry Politics**

The #MeToo movement revealed the toxic culture within many traditional studios. By working with streaming platforms or starting their own companies, stars can create safer, more inclusive environments.

Hi, I’m Lara, a passionate storyteller, actress and entrepreneur. From stage to screen, pen to palette, I'm a creative chameleon on a never-ending quest for inspiration.

I​​’m someone who thrives where creativity meets connection. Over the past decade, I’ve worn many hats—entrepreneur, storyteller, teacher, actress, writer—and each of them has shaped my approach to work and life in unique ways.

At the core of everything I do is a drive to inspire, create, and connect. I believe in the power of stories to shape how we see the world—and I’m here to keep telling them, whether through fashion, words, or beyond.

--- SOURCE 3: Netflix Originals: Dissecting the Success of Streaming Services and ... ---
URL: https://krock.io/blog/made-in-krock/netflix-originals-dissecting-the-success-of-streaming-services-and-their-impact-on-filmmaking/

SUMMARY:
Home > Blog > Made in Krock > Netflix Originals: Dissecting the Success of Streaming Services and Their Impact on Filmmaking In this article, we will dive deep into the success of Netflix Originals, examining how streaming services have disrupted traditional models and reshaped the landscape of the film industry. ## The Future Of Film Industry In Light Of The Rise Of Netflix Originals And Other Streaming Platforms The future may witness a restructuring of the industry as studios, streaming platforms, and independent filmmakers find new ways to collaborate and distribute content effectively. * The Future Of Film Industry In Light Of The Rise Of Netflix Originals And Other Streaming Platforms

FULL CONTENT:
[Skip to content](#content)

[Home](https://krock.io/) > [Blog](https://krock.io/category/blog/) > [Made in Krock](https://krock.io/category/blog/made-in-krock/) > Netflix Originals: Dissecting the Success of Streaming Services and Their Impact on Filmmaking

Amina

Amina

Share with your community!

In the past decade, the entertainment industry has undergone a significant transformation, thanks to the rise of streaming services. Platforms like Netflix have revolutionized the way we consume content, making it accessible to millions of viewers worldwide at the click of a button.

One of the most notable aspects of this streaming revolution is the emergence of Netflix Originals, a category of content produced exclusively for the platform. These Originals have not only captured audiences’ attention but have also left an indelible mark on the world of filmmaking. In this article, we will dive deep into the success of Netflix Originals, examining how streaming services have disrupted traditional models and reshaped the landscape of the film industry.

Netflix, founded in **1997** as a DVD rental service, took a significant leap forward in 2007 by introducing its streaming service. This move paved the way for a new era in entertainment, forever changing the dynamics between studios, filmmakers, and viewers. As the streaming giant expanded its user base, it began to produce its own content, giving birth to Netflix Originals.

This move was met with skepticism and doubt from industry insiders, who questioned whether an online platform could compete with traditional film studios. However, Netflix’s strategic approach to content creation soon proved its detractors wrong.

One of the key factors behind the success of Netflix Originals is the creative freedom granted to filmmakers. Unlike traditional studios, Netflix provides its creators with a unique opportunity to explore unconventional ideas, take risks, and push boundaries. This creative autonomy has attracted some of the industry’s most talented directors, writers, and actors, who see Netflix as a platform where their vision can truly come to life. As a result, audiences have been treated to a diverse array of stories that might have otherwise gone unnoticed or untold.

Another crucial aspect of the success of Netflix Originals is the platform’s global reach. With a presence in over 190 countries, Netflix has a truly international audience. This global accessibility has opened up opportunities for filmmakers from different cultures and backgrounds to showcase their work to a worldwide audience. By producing localized content and partnering with international talent, Netflix has created a cultural exchange of ideas and narratives that transcends borders. This approach not only broadens the horizons of viewers but also fosters a sense of inclusivity and representation in storytelling.

Netflix’s data-driven approach to content creation is yet another aspect that sets it apart from traditional studios. By analyzing user behavior, viewing patterns, and preferences, Netflix is able to identify niche genres and underserved audiences. This valuable data enables the platform to develop content tailored to specific viewer segments, leading to a higher chance of success.

This data-centric strategy has revolutionized the way content is greenlit, minimizing the risk associated with traditional film production. Filmmakers can now rely on concrete insights to validate their ideas, allowing for a more diverse range of content to thrive.

The success of Netflix Originals has also disrupted the distribution model of the film industry. Traditionally, a film’s success relied heavily on its theatrical release. However, Netflix’s streaming platform eliminates the constraints of time and physical space. Viewers can access the content at their convenience, and filmmakers no longer have to rely solely on box office numbers to measure their success.

This shift has empowered filmmakers to experiment with new formats, such as limited series or episodic storytelling, without being confined by the limitations of a traditional cinematic experience. The ability to binge-watch entire seasons or discover hidden gems long after their release has allowed for a more organic and sustained viewership.

Netflix’s commitment to innovation extends beyond content creation and distribution. The platform has embraced emerging technologies, such as virtual reality and interactive storytelling, to enhance the viewer experience. In 2018, Netflix released **“Black Mirror: Bandersnatch,”** an interactive film that allowed viewers to choose the narrative’s direction by making decisions on behalf of the protagonist.

This groundbreaking experiment in interactive storytelling showcased the platform’s willingness to push boundaries and explore new ways of engaging audiences. By embracing these technologies, Netflix has become a pioneer in the future of filmmaking, blurring the line between traditional storytelling and immersive experiences.

While the success of Netflix Originals is undeniable, it has not been without its controversies. The streaming giant’s dominance and deep pockets have raised concerns among some filmmakers and industry professionals. Netflix’s disruptive business model, which often involves bypassing traditional distribution channels, has sparked debates about the future of cinema and the role of streaming services in the industry.

Filmmakers who advocate for the theatrical experience worry that the rise of streaming services could diminish the value of the big screen and the communal viewing experience it offers. Additionally, there have been discussions about fair compensation for creators, as streaming platforms operate under different financial models compared to traditional studios.  
Nevertheless, the impact of streaming services on the film industry cannot be denied.

Netflix Originals have not only redefined how content is created, distributed, and consumed but have also challenged the traditional power structures within the industry. With its immense global audience and data-driven approach, Netflix has become a major player in shaping the future of filmmaking. Furthermore, the success of Netflix Originals has inspired other streaming platforms, such as Amazon Prime Video and Hulu, to invest in their own original content, further diversifying the streaming landscape.

## The Business Model Behind Netflix Originals: How They are Changing the Game

**Subscription-based model:** Netflix operates on a subscription-based model, where users pay a monthly fee to access the entire library of content, including Netflix Originals. This model allows Netflix to generate a steady and predictable stream of revenue, enabling them to invest heavily in creating original content.

**Data-driven content creation:** Netflix leverages its vast amount of user data to make data-driven decisions about content creation. They analyze viewership patterns, preferences, and feedback to identify potential ideas and genres that are likely to resonate with their audience. This data-driven approach helps them minimize risk and increase the chances of creating successful shows.

**Vertical integration:** Netflix has adopted a vertical integration strategy by becoming a content creator and distributor. By producing their own content, they have control over the entire creative process and can tailor it to their platform and audience. This eliminates the need to rely solely on third-party content providers, giving them more flexibility and creative freedom.

**Global reach:** Netflix operates in multiple countries worldwide, and its original content is available globally. This global reach allows them to tap into diverse markets and cater to a wide range of audiences. By producing content in various languages and exploring stories from different cultures, Netflix can attract a global subscriber base.

**Content binge-watching:** Netflix Originals are often released in a binge-friendly format, where entire seasons or series are made available at once. This departure from traditional weekly episode releases caters to the changing viewing habits of audiences who prefer to consume content at their own pace. Binge-watching encourages longer engagement periods and increases customer satisfaction and loyalty.

**Creative freedom and risk-taking:** Netflix provides creators with creative freedom and takes risks on unconventional or niche content that traditional media companies might shy away from. This approach has led to the production of unique and critically acclaimed shows and movies that have garnered a dedicated fan base. By supporting innovative storytelling, Netflix has been able to differentiate itself in the crowded entertainment landscape.

**Financial investment in content:** Netflix invests heavily in content creation, dedicating a significant portion of its budget to producing original programming. They allocate billions of dollars each year to acquire and produce high-quality content. This substantial investment has allowed them to attract top talent, create visually stunning productions, and compete with traditional studios and networks.

**Marketing and promotion:** Netflix employs sophisticated marketing and promotion strategies to build anticipation and awareness around its Originals. They utilize data-driven insights to target specific audiences and create personalized marketing campaigns. They also capitalize on social media platforms, influencers, and word-of-mouth to generate buzz and drive viewership.

## The Future Of Film Industry In Light Of The Rise Of Netflix Originals And Other Streaming Platforms

### Evolving Distribution Strategies

The traditional model of releasing films exclusively in theaters is being challenged by the popularity of streaming platforms. The future of the film industry will likely see a continued shift toward simultaneous or staggered releases, where films are made available both in theaters and on streaming platforms. This hybrid distribution approach allows filmmakers to reach a wider audience while still capitalizing on the cinematic experience.

### Increased Competition and Content Diversity

Streaming platforms like Netflix, Amazon Prime Video, Hulu, and Disney+ have aggressively entered the original content space. This competition has led to a surge in content diversity and quality, with filmmakers having more opportunities to create and showcase their work. As a result, audiences can expect a broader range of stories, genres, and voices in the film industry.

### Impact on the Traditional Studio System

The dominance of streaming platforms has challenged the traditional studio system. Major studios are now investing in their streaming platforms or forming partnerships with existing ones to adapt to changing viewer preferences. The future may witness a restructuring of the industry as studios, streaming platforms, and independent filmmakers find new ways to collaborate and distribute content effectively.

### Audience Viewing Habits and Cinema Experience

The rise of streaming platforms has changed how audiences consume films. While many still enjoy the communal experience of watching movies in theaters, the convenience and accessibility of streaming services have fueled a rise in at-home viewing. In response, theaters may focus more on enhancing the cinematic experience with premium formats, immersive technologies, and unique offerings that differentiate them from home viewing.

### Global Reach and Cross-Cultural Storytelling

Streaming platforms have a global reach, allowing films to reach audiences worldwide without the traditional barriers of international distribution. This enables filmmakers to explore cross-cultural storytelling, facilitating the exchange of ideas, narratives, and perspectives from different regions. The future of the film industry will likely see increased collaboration and co-productions across borders, resulting in a more interconnected global cinematic landscape.

### Data-driven Decision-making

Streaming platforms heavily rely on data analytics to understand viewer preferences, engagement patterns, and content performance. This data-driven approach will likely influence the decision-making process for filmmakers and studios. Analyzing data insights can help shape content creation, marketing strategies, and audience targeting, leading to more targeted and personalized film experiences.

### Alternative Financing Models

Streaming platforms have disrupted traditional financing models by offering significant budgets for original content. Filmmakers can now explore alternative financing avenues beyond traditional studios, attracting investors, independent production companies, and streaming platforms themselves. This increased funding diversity opens doors for more innovative and risk-taking projects.

## Conclusion

Netflix Originals have proven to be a game-changer in the world of filmmaking. By granting creative freedom, embracing global diversity, leveraging data insights, and exploring new technologies, Netflix has transformed the way stories are told and consumed.

While the rise of streaming services has brought about its fair share of debates and challenges, it has undoubtedly opened up new opportunities for filmmakers, allowing them to reach a broader audience and bring their unique visions to life. As the streaming revolution continues to evolve, it will be fascinating to witness how it shapes the future of the film industry and the stories we tell.

## Streaming Platforms and Their Impact on the Film Industry For better or worse, streaming has significantly impacted the film industry in terms of how films are produced and distributed and how audiences consume them. Streaming services have become movie-making powerhouses – Netflix alone has produced over 6,000 original titles, including television series, documentaries, movies, stand-up specials, and interactive specials. Should streaming services continue investing in original content production, this could lead to an even greater diversity of voices and perspectives represented in the entertainment industry. Introducing our exclusive 2-ebook bundle, Financials in Rights Management, a treasure trove of insights designed to amplify your content's reach and profitability across the streaming landscape while

FULL CONTENT:
## Streaming Platforms and Their Impact on the Film Industry

[Back to INDUSTRY INSIGHTS](https://blog.filmtrack.com/industry-insights#insights-grid-section)

When VHS was introduced in 1977 and DVDs burst onto the scene 20 years later, consumers were presented with the first viable alternatives to a movie theater. Though seen as revolutionary then, watching movies at home and bypassing theaters continues to grow. Streaming services have been available since 2005 when YouTube burst on the scene, and its pickup continues to increase sharply, particularly in the past few years. Over the last decade, movie theater attendance has declined sharply, and the list of streaming services seemingly grows every day.  

The film industry is undergoing radical change. [In 2018, 28%](https://www.thedartmouth.com/article/2022/10/trends-streaming-services#:~:text=Over%20the%20last%20decade%2C%20interest,and%20damages%20the%20industry%20irrevocably.) of consumers preferred to watch a movie for the first time in a movie theater, compared to only 15% who favored streaming. However, COVID-19 devastated theaters due to closures and the apprehension people felt about returning to theaters after the lockdown. Case in point: In 2020, [some theaters lost billions](https://variety.com/2021/film/news/amc-theatres-4-6-billion-loss-covid-1234927642/) after closing their doors due to the pandemic. By June 2020, the percentage of adults who strongly preferred seeing a movie in a theater [fell to 14%, with 36%](https://www.statista.com/statistics/947757/theaters-streaming-watching-movies/#:~:text=According%20to%20a%20study%20held,home%20than%20visit%20a%20cinema.) choosing to stream movies instead. 

For better or worse, streaming has significantly impacted the film industry in terms of how films are produced and distributed and how audiences consume them.

## How Streaming has Impacted Production and Distribution

Streaming services facilitate the production and distribution of more diverse and niche content. In addition, because they are not as limited by the traditional Hollywood studio system, streaming services can take more chances on relatively unknown filmmakers and projects, which might have a more challenging time securing funding or distribution through conventional channels. A broader range of voices and perspectives are now represented in film and television. It has made it easier for underrepresented groups to find content that echoes their experiences.

In our post, [*The Evolution of Storytelling and its Impact on Rights Management*](/industry-insights/the-evolution-of-storytelling-and-its-impact-on-rights-management), we discuss how the pandemic accelerated changes that have been coming for years, such as the collapse of the theatrical window and moving to video-on-demand, into a relatively short period of time. Now every major player has a video-on-demand platform, whether it’s a sell-through platform like iTunes, where users can purchase something to watch, a subscription-based platform like Netflix or Amazon Prime, or a combination of both.

With more and more people choosing to view films and television shows online, the number of people going to movie theaters has dropped, leading to a decline in box office sales. Television networks are also losing audience numbers as more consumers get entertainment via streaming services. However, although streaming services have led to a drop in traditional revenue, they have also introduced new revenue sources, such as subscription fees and advertising tailored directly to viewers’ interests.

## How Streaming has Impacted the Audience

Streaming services like Netflix, Amazon Prime, and Hulu have transformed media consumption. Instead of going to a physical location to rent or purchase a movie or television show, consumers can now access a vast library of content online with just a few clicks. As a result, practices like binge-watching have become common. 

One of the drivers of this change could be price: The average cost of a movie ticket at the theater is [$10.45](https://www.the-numbers.com/market/), compared to [$6.99 for a basic monthly Netflix subscription](https://www.businessinsider.com/guides/streaming/netflix-price#:~:text=A%20Netflix%20monthly%20subscription%20costs,at%20a%20time%20for%20%2419.99.). Thus, some view the growing preference for streaming services as a positive - movies are becoming more widely available and accessible for everyone.

However, streaming has also significantly impacted viewer experience and entertainment culture. Since films and television shows can be watched online, the traditional experience of traveling to a theater, purchasing a ticket, and watching a movie on a big screen is becoming less common, lessening the sense of community that watching a film with others provides.

## How Streaming Might Impact the Future of the Film Industry

Streaming services have become movie-making powerhouses – Netflix alone has produced over [6,000 original titles](https://www.whats-on-netflix.com/news/50-of-netflixs-library-is-now-made-of-netflix-originals/), including television series, documentaries, movies, stand-up specials, and interactive specials. According to Netflix, should the company continue this trajectory, it will likely hit the following milestones in the next few years:

* [July 2023: 60%](https://www.whats-on-netflix.com/news/50-of-netflixs-library-is-now-made-of-netflix-originals/) Netflix originals vs. licensed content
* [December 2024: 75%](https://www.whats-on-netflix.com/news/50-of-netflixs-library-is-now-made-of-netflix-originals/) Netflix originals vs. licensed content
* [May 2027: 100%](https://www.whats-on-netflix.com/news/50-of-netflixs-library-is-now-made-of-netflix-originals/) Netflix originals vs. licensed content

Streaming services are now challenging traditional studios as prolific producers of films and television shows. Should streaming services continue investing in original content production, this could lead to an even greater diversity of voices and perspectives represented in the entertainment industry. As these services grow in popularity, their impact on the film industry will continue to evolve, requiring traditional distribution channels to adapt and find ways to coexist.

## The Evolution of Film & TV Rights

Entertainment rights management has become increasingly fraught with complexity in tracking various rights and royalties. Our ebook, [*Complex Film & TV Rights Management: History, Current Tracking, and 21st Century Solutions*](https://info.filmtrack.com/complex-film-and-tv-rights-management-ebook), explores this evolution and looks at what a modern rights management platform should give you and how to shop for one. Dive in and download the ebook!

Increase revenue, get to market quicker, and optimize the value of your library. Our Contracts and Availabilities Manager provides automated, real-time contract and availabilities data across your entire catalog.

#### FilmTrack's Financials Manager

Enjoy the most robust financial tools in the industry, built to handle the most complex calculations. Whether it’s circular deductions, financial waterfalls, or royalties in/out, you can relax and know that everything is being split and paid accurately.

[Learn more](https://www.filmtrack.com/financials-manager)

SUMMARY:
Netflix's SVOD model has also disrupted traditional film and TV production. The platform has shifted the power dynamics within the industry,

FULL CONTENT:
![AlphansoTech Blog](//www.alphansotech.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/cropped-logo-1.png)
![AlphansoTech Blog](//www.alphansotech.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/cropped-logo-1.png)

# How is SVOD Netflix Transforming Television and Film Industries?

![How is SVOD Netflix Transforming Television and Film Industries?](https://www.alphansotech.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/SVOD-Netflix.png)

In the past decade, Netflix has evolved from a DVD rental service to a dominant player in the Subscription Video on Demand (SVOD) industry, reshaping how we consume entertainment. As the flagship of [SVOD platforms](https://www.alphansotech.com/blog/top-svod-platforms-in-2024-comparative-analysis/), Netflix’s influence has reached every corner of the globe, radically transforming the television and film industries. From production techniques to viewing habits, Netflix’s innovations have ushered in a new era for content creation and consumption. Let’s explore the profound ways in which a Netflix SVOD model has impacted the global entertainment landscape.

Table of Contents

## What is the SVOD Netflix Model?

The SVOD Netflix Model refers to Netflix’s unique approach to offering Subscription Video-on-demand (SVOD), where subscribers pay a recurring fee to access a vast library of content that can be streamed on demand. Netflix pioneered this model and continues to dominate the market by evolving it with innovative strategies.

## Netflix to Remain SVOD Leader Globally

The global SVOD market is expected to see significant growth in the coming years, with a projected increase of 475 million subscriptions between 2021 and 2027, bringing the total to 1.68 billion. Despite the rise of numerous competitors, Netflix is forecasted to maintain its dominant position as the global leader in SVOD revenues.

Statista report shows that the quarterly number of [Netflix streaming subscribers](https://www.statista.com/statistics/250934/quarterly-number-of-netflix-streaming-subscribers-worldwide/) is increasing steadily. As of the second quarter of 2024, Netflix has almost 277 million paid subscribers worldwide. Most subscribers are based in the EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) region, around 94 million of Netflix’s total subscriber base.

### Continued Global Dominance

By 2027, six U.S.-based platforms—led by Netflix—will account for nearly 47% of the world’s total SVOD subscriptions. As the largest player, Netflix will continue to leverage its massive subscriber base and international reach to remain a key force in the industry.

### SVOD Subscription Growth

Although competitors like Disney+ are growing rapidly, with forecasts initially predicting it would surpass Netflix by 2027, the landscape has changed.

![Global SVOD Subscribers by Platform](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfKxQ9usU1fDNXfUs5oCVpqR5WNON6IoekCo8sVFOXcxNK8ZpJiMbUMRbDCJmH1YRPxsi1cm5yT83XAi5R1q4fklHQlsDdjHFKX-0M7mqoE9AH8EanvpFWeQEyuldRDeR4y4Ej-xVptSfK7sZ9jh5mCnRBY?key=y8af_A_XKrakmcwar3Z-Ew)

**Source: (**[**DigitalTV Research**](https://digitaltvresearch.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/SVOD-Forecasts-Update-0922-TOC.pdf)**)**

According to Simon Murray, Principal Analyst at Digital TV Research, Disney+ is expected to have [274 million subscribers](https://digitaltvresearch.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/SVOD-Forecasts-Update-0922-TOC.pdf) by 2027, while Netflix is projected to maintain 253 million subscribers. Disney+’s projected growth was revised downward due to the loss of the India Premier League cricket rights, which led to a forecast reduction of 67 million subscribers.

### Revenue Leadership

In terms of revenue, Netflix is poised to remain the top earner in the SVOD space. By 2027, Netflix is expected to generate $30 billion in revenue—more than Disney+, HBO Max, and Paramount+ combined.

The introduction of hybrid AVOD-SVOD tiers by Disney+ and other platforms will increase ARPU (average revenue per user), but Netflix’s established global presence and pricing power will ensure it remains the highest-revenue-generating platform in the world.

### Global SVOD Market Outlook

Overall, the global [SVOD market](https://www.gminsights.com/industry-analysis/svod-market) is expected to grow at 11% CAGR between 2024 and 2032. The total revenues will reach 127 billion U.S. dollars by 2029.

As consumer demand for on-demand content continues to rise, Netflix’s focus on producing high-quality, localized content and its ability to cater to diverse international markets will enable it to maintain its position as the SVOD leader worldwide.

## SVOD Netflix’s Global Impact: Transforming Television and Film Industries

One of the most profound impacts of Netflix’s SVOD model has been the way it redefined content consumption. Traditionally, viewers were bound to scheduled programming on cable television, with limited control over what and when they could watch. Netflix broke this mold by offering an on-demand content library that could be accessed anytime, from anywhere.

As of 2024, Netflix boasts over 238 million global subscribers across more than 190 countries, signaling a massive shift from [traditional TV](https://www.alphansotech.com/blog/free-ad-supported-tv-is-growing-faster-explore-how/) to digital streaming. Its success has prompted a surge of SVOD platforms, each vying to replicate Netflix’s model.

The ‘binge-watching’ culture, which was popularized by Netflix’s release of entire seasons of shows at once, transformed viewers’ habits and established a new era in TV and film consumption.

### Democratizing Content Creation: Global Reach, Local Stories

Netflix has democratized content creation in ways unimaginable a decade ago. With its deep pockets and global reach, the platform has actively sought out local stories from around the world, giving creators from diverse cultures a chance to tell their stories on a massive stage.

Netflix’s push into foreign markets has been instrumental in the rise of non-English content. Series like **Money Heist (Spain)**, **Dark (Germany)**,and **Squid Game (South Korea)** have become global sensations, proving that language is no longer a barrier to success in entertainment. Squid Game became Netflix’s most-watched series in 2021, illustrating how powerful non-English content can be when given a global platform.

This embrace of diverse content has also fostered cultural exchange. Audiences in the U.S. and Europe are now as likely to binge-watch a Korean drama or a French thriller as they are an American sitcom.

An SVOD [platform like Netflix](https://www.alphansotech.com/netflix-clone) opens up opportunities for international creators, establishing a new era where stories can cross borders and gain worldwide recognition, with the potential for massive success.

### Challenging Traditional Film and TV Production

Netflix’s SVOD model has also disrupted traditional film and TV production. The platform has shifted the power dynamics within the industry, challenging the traditional studio system. By committing billions of dollars to content creation, Netflix has become a significant player in original programming, producing everything from blockbuster films to niche documentaries.

One of the most notable examples of Netflix’s power to disrupt was its acquisition and production of Roma (2018), a film by Alfonso Cuarón. Not only did Roma win three Academy Awards, but it also sparked a debate about whether streaming services should compete in major film festivals and award shows. The film’s success forced traditional film studios and awards committees to recognize that Netflix—and other streaming platforms—are not only viable but formidable players in the entertainment industry.

Netflix’s production model has also shifted the emphasis from box office revenue to subscriber growth. With its vast audience and unique data insights into viewer preferences, Netflix can greenlight projects that might have been deemed too risky by traditional studios.

The ability to test and develop content based on data-driven decisions allows Netflix to experiment with new formats and genres, from interactive content like Black Mirror: Bandersnatch to choose-your-own-adventure specials for kids.

### Platform for Creators: Flexibility and Innovation

Beyond content consumption, Netflix’s SVOD platform has empowered creators by offering unparalleled flexibility. Filmmakers, writers, and producers are no longer bound by the constraints of network television or theatrical release schedules. Netflix allows creators the freedom to explore longer or shorter formats, experiment with unconventional storytelling techniques, and directly reach a global audience without having to navigate the complexities of traditional distribution channels.

Netflix’s focus on creating original [streaming content](https://www.alphansotech.com/blog/personalized-recommendations-how-it-helps-to-offer-tailored-streaming-content/) has turned it into a sought-after partner for top-tier talent, while also supporting the rise of indie filmmakers and niche creators. By offering competitive budgets and creative freedom, Netflix has allowed artists to push the boundaries of storytelling.

This freedom, combined with a global distribution platform, has led to a wave of innovation in content creation, from boundary-pushing TV series like Stranger Things to award-winning documentaries like Tiger King. The platform’s impact has redefined the television industry, where it is no longer just about viewership ratings but about the broader influence and staying power of its shows.

## Future of SVOD Netflix: Competition and Challenges

While Netflix has undoubtedly transformed the global entertainment industry, it is not without challenges. The rise of competitors like Disney+, Amazon Prime Video, and HBO Max has intensified the battle for subscriber dominance.

These competitors have begun pulling their content from Netflix, forcing the platform to double down on original content production. Netflix’s increasing focus on producing its own blockbuster films and series is its response to this growing competition.

Furthermore, the SVOD market has started to fragment, and consumers are facing subscription fatigue. With an increasing number of platforms demanding monthly fees, viewers are starting to pick and choose, sometimes canceling Netflix in favor of new entrants.

However, Netflix continues to evolve. The platform is experimenting with [advertising-based subscription models](https://www.alphansotech.com/blog/what-is-avod-advertising-video-on-demand-comprehensive-guide/) and gaming integrations, diversifying its content beyond traditional film and TV. The company has also entered the live sports space, expanding its portfolio to cater to the evolving demands of the modern audience.

## Want to Launch Your SVOD Netflix Platform?

Netflix’s SVOD model has dramatically transformed the television and film industries. By disrupting traditional distribution, creating a global marketplace for content, and pushing the boundaries of what’s possible in storytelling, Netflix has reshaped the entertainment landscape.

As the competition heats up, it will be fascinating to see how Netflix continues to innovate and maintain its role as a leader in the global entertainment industry. Regardless of the challenges, Netflix’s impact on how we watch, produce, and engage with content is undeniable—and it will be felt for generations to come.

## Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)

**Why is Netflix’s original content strategy significant?**

Netflix’s investment in original content helps the platform stand out, ensuring it doesn’t rely solely on third-party licenses.

**How has Netflix contributed to the popularity of binge-watching?**

Netflix popularized the concept of binge-watching by releasing entire seasons of shows at once. This approach allows viewers to watch multiple episodes or entire series in one sitting, shifting the way audiences consume TV content compared to traditional weekly episode releases.

**How does Netflix’s global approach benefit viewers & content creators?**

Netflix’s global approach means viewers have access to a wide variety of content from different regions, promoting cross-cultural understanding. For content creators, it opens doors to reach international audiences, which previously was challenging under traditional TV distribution methods.
